### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-05-21
Just so you are aware, I believe there is a bug in the Envy Unstable script.

 I troubleshot the API mismatch for about 2 hours with the help of a forum user that goes by the screen name psyke83 or somethign very similar to that. HE was helping me thru gaim (IM). 

He tried to have me add blacklist nvidia_new to the /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist file, BUT that still didn't work, NO module was loading when we tried that and that fix DID work for psyke83 so we weren't sure why it didn't work on my machine? I also ensure that my /etc/defaults/linux-blah-blah-blah-common DID have the Disabled MOdules = "nv" within it and it STILL DIDN'T WORKL.

It turned out that the Envy unstable program had a bug and didn't do somethign correctly (if this is an untrue statement, I am only conveying the message that psyke83 informed me of. He said that he tried telling the author of Envy but the author of Envy said there was no bug. Then psyke83 even tried the unstable version on his own computer and tried to install 100.xx.xx and he got the same API mismatch error). 

So the soltuion was to add a hack to my rc.local file so that the correct nvidia kernel module would load. This is what I added BEFORE the exit 0 within the rc.local file:
find /lib/modules/`uname -r` | grep -i nvi | grep -v fb | grep nvidia.ko$ | xargs insmod
and finally the damn 100.xx.xx kernel module would load. BUT to no avail, random freezing is STILL OCCURING!!!! I have posted a million times in various threads about Fiesty freezing on simple tasks, like gedit, or various config dialog boxes opened thru System, Admin or Prefs. ANyway, I thoguht I'd at least let other know about the solution if they get teh dreaded API mismatch error when the nvidia driver is 100.xx.xx but the module being loaded was 9755 when they used Envy Unstable.

The FGLRX drivers currently don't support XOrg 7.2 (1.3) at all so that's why you'll have issues. Someone put up a hack on Launchpad though that alters the files to allow them to run so now I'm able to use XOrg 7.2 on my laptop with the 8.36.x driver.

### Post by dodgePT on 2007-06-01
In phoronix.net:

> It was a year ago that AMD/ATI had delivered the fglrx 8.25.18 display driver, which corrected a bug causing issues with the Radeon X1000 512MB video cards, added new product support, and squashed a variety of other bugs. Well, today AMD delivered the 8.37.6 Linux display driver. Affecting this driver the most is support for X server version 1.3 and appended several other fixes along with a slight upgrade to the AMD Catalyst Control Center Linux Edition. While no AIGLX support was added or no new features introduced, the upgrade should be worth the time.

The fixes for the 8.37.6 driver includes proper swapping on Vsync, translation updates in the AMD Catalyst Control Center, and the TV-Out video is no longer cropped when using X-Video. Also introduced in 8.37.6 is Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 packaging support provided by AMD. Red Hat Enterprise Linux, Red Flag, and Novell's SuSE are the distributions officially supported by AMD, and thus we now have support for RHEL 5 with the fglrx drivers. There are also a few improvements in the Linux Catalyst Control Center outside of the translation updates.

The AMD Catalyst Control Center Linux Edition is now officially at version 1.0. In addition to the translation improvements, the 3D page has finally been added. Anti-aliasing (AA) and Anisotropic Filtering (AF) can finally be adjusted through AMD's GUI control panel. The anti-aliasing levels supported are 2x, 4x, and 6x with the ability to override any application settings. The Anisotropic Filtering levels automatically override application settings and the levels supported here are 2x, 4x, 8x, and 16x. The 3D page also supports 8-bit double-buffered overlay planes and waiting for vertical retraces.

[IMG]http://www.phoronix.net/image.php?id=734&image=amd_837_ccc[/IMG]

This driver does not append support for the Radeon HD 2000 series "R600", but the product support will hopefully arrive soon.

Maybe some light at the end of the tunnel?

fglrx: module license 'Proprietary. (C) 2002 - ATI Technologies, Starnberg, GERMANY' taints kernel.

[fglrx:firegl_init_module] *ERROR* firegl_stub_register failed

1.) I am not familiar with ATI and Envy, only Nvidia so I can maybe speculate where to find a log file for Xorg in general but not an Envy log file. Check in /var/log/Xorg.0.log to see what it's saying about why your xserver failed to launch.
2.) I would say that you have already answered your question. Both ways would/should work. If all else fails, you could always use "vesa" as the xorg.conf driver, which would at least get ya back into your x-server and your Desktop (GUI)
3.) No, you're not crazy BUT yes, you did enter a password when you installed Feisty. It's part of the Install. The sudo password is the same as YOUR usernames password BUT this is different than the ROOT password. Ubuntu does NOT have a root password by default, it even has root login disabled. If you want to create a root password (you don't need to however, you can switch to the root user by issuing sudo -i, then your usernames password OR you can always just use sudo. NOTE: some commands can't be done with sudo and you need to swtich to root to do them) you would use this command:
sudo passwd root
then enter the desired root password twice. this still doesn't allow root to login, that's done frmo the login preferences within the System, Prefs pulldown somewhere, I think btu again, you really would never need to login as root, you can always switch to root) If you really don't know your own password (sudo password), then you'll have to boot into a live cd, chroot into your installed Feisty install, and issue this command:
sudo passwd yourusernamehere
then enter the new desired password twice, then that will be the password for your user the next time you login. If you don't know how to chroot into your installed feisty, then gogle it. there's guides everywhere.

---

### Post by Alex Fernandez on 2007-06-08

name of display: :0.0
display: :0  screen: 0
direct rendering: Yes


Actually, it all works.


dannyboy, well after upgrading from Feisty to Gutsy devel other day all I did was follow the same ATi install procedure as I've done before (for the latest drivers from their site).

```

bash <ati.run.file> --buildpkg Ubuntu/gutsy
sudo dpkg -i *.deb
sudo rm -fr /usr/src/fglrx-kernel*.deb
sudo apt-get -f install
sudo module-assistant prepare
sudo module-assistant update
sudo module-assistant build fglrx
sudo module-assistant install fglrx
sudo depmod -ae

```

Notice that I'm building a gutsy package now, instead of a feisty one though.

### Post by tseliot on 2007-06-19
**UPDATE**: if you have experienced the **API mismatch problem** with **Envy**, please follow these steps:
```
sudo apt-get --purge remove envy
```
```
sudo rm -R /usr/share/envy
```

then get the latest release of Envy.

Hi,

I have been doing my best to find a solution to this problem. Lets see if here. I was given an advice to try Envy on my native ubuntu forum, and I love the concept behind Envy, but encoutered some rather interesting problems.

Some background info: the problem started after the computer was in the maintenance, and the graphic card was switched from Ati Radeon X.600 to X.700se (as they claim), but the diagnostic says it is X.1600series. At any rate, everything worked just fine before (including direct rendering).

After I got the computer back, I noticed that direct rendering is not working. So, I followed the advice given in ubuntu's wikipedia to get it back. To no avail. Then I used the graphical interface (maintenance-restricted drivers maintenance) and it said I had restricted drivers installed but not in use. So I ticked it to uninstall them, and re-installed them, reboot and direct rendering started to work...

Some interesting aspects here: with open-source drivers that come with the ubuntu, the GUI of X won't start and throws me back to the shell.  So back to the restricted.

So I install the restricted drivers, see everything is OK (fglrxinfo etc.)

Then I run envy to install ATI's latest drivers....everything goes well, but what happens is this:

```

kotka@Imagineer:~$ fglrxinfo

Xlib:  extension "XFree86-DRI" missing on display ":0.0".
display: :0.0  screen: 0

direct rendering: No

kotka@Imagineer:~$

### Post by jbaloul on 2007-06-22
**This post could be related to an Ubuntu bug filed at**: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/nvidia-kernel-common/+bug/109721](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/nvidia-kernel-common/+bug/109721) [br].[/br] ---------------------------- [br].[/br] [br].[/br]
				Hi Guys,

Seems like i am not the only one so i added it to the bug in launchpad as well:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.20/+bug/107110](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux-restricted-modules-2.6.20/+bug/107110)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/nvidia-kernel-common/+bug/109721](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/nvidia-kernel-common/+bug/109721)

I managed to overcome all the Dual-Display Twinview Problem however...
GLX /Xgl still crashes horribly...

I tried the 2.6.20-16 & 2.6.20-15 kernels with all proprietary & non-proprietary Nvidia drivers...Xgl goes to 100% CPU and dies.
So although we still have dual display, no more compiz nor beryl in the twinview config.

To Reproduce:
Stop all X instances:
/etc/init.d/xorg-common stop && /etc/init.d/gdm stop
or
/etc/init.d/xorg-common stop && /etc/init.d/kdm stop #for kubuntu
then in the console run:
export DISPLAY=:1
sudo /usr/bin/Xgl :1 -fullscreen +xinerama &
glxgears &

...after a few seconds of spinning the Gears will slow down, XGL will go to 100% CPU and a few more mouse clicks will kill the X session completly.

heres some of my system info:
lspci |grep -i nvid
01:05.0 VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation NV43 [GeForce 6600/GeForce 6600 GT] (rev a2)

 modprobe -l |grep -i nvid
/lib/modules/2.6.20-16-generic/kernel/drivers/video/nvidia/nvidiafb.ko
/lib/modules/2.6.20-16-generic/kernel/drivers/char/agp/nvidia-agp.ko
/lib/modules/2.6.20-16-generic/volatile/nvidia.ko
/lib/modules/2.6.20-16-generic/volatile/nvidia_legacy.ko
/lib/modules/2.6.20-16-generic/volatile/nvidia_new.ko
Kernel:
2.6.20-16-generic #2 SMP
xserver-xorg 7.2-0ubuntu11
Feisty

--
thanks,
Jacob
[http://howtoforums.net](http://howtoforums.net)

Also, if you read the guide, it does state that this is an old thread which was for Dapper and Edgy. The new thread within this forum is here: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=432056](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=432056)

If you don't have an Nvidia 8800 but still a pretty new card (here's the compatibility chart for which hardware works with which driver: [http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/1.0-9755/README/appendix-a.html](http://us.download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/1.0-9755/README/appendix-a.html)) than I suggest using the normal repositories and install nvidia-glx-new (this is proprietary nvidia driver version 9755), make sure you open the file located within /etc/defaults/linux-restricted-modules and make sure that you put nv within the quotes so it looks like this "nv", then save than file, then restart X with ctrl-alt-backspace, and you should be set, if you don't get into X, then you have other nvidia's drivers conflicting and you need to remove and purge all nvidia stuff. Run nvidia-settings to get the best resolution and make sure you save your new xorg.conf after changing stuff within nvidia-settings.

Here's a wiki on it also: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/Nvidia?highlight=%28nvidia%29](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/Nvidia?highlight=%28nvidia%29)

---

### Post by dannyboy79 on 2007-07-27
I would strongly suggest reading some in the future BUT I can help you this time. It's just that something like "using the multiverse repositories" is something you should've already learned how to enable. Everything you can imagine on how to do is all here: [http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu:Feisty](http://ubuntuguide.org/wiki/Ubuntu:Feisty)

As far as how to install the driver, that's what this thread was for, you could just read a few pages and find out what works for people but I'll help you this time. You have a 7300 according to your sig. You need to simply enable the "extra repositories" per the link above, then simply go to aptitude (package manager) and search for and install the Nvidia Driver 9755, that's the latest stable release. That should auto update your xorg.conf if I remember right, I haven't done it in so long. IF by installing the 9755 thru synaptic doesn't work, then you must have tried the restricted manager (which didn't work for me either) and you now need to follow this link:
Follow the steps in post number 78 here ([http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=432056&page=8](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=432056&page=8)) for your specific card and driver.

Good luck

### Post by NT4usB on 2007-07-28
Awright... enough fighting the ATi card and Mythtv. fglrx works fine but MythTV hates it.
Out with the ATi and in with a GeForce 7600 GS.
Here's what I did:

Remove purged envy and installed the latest.
Swap the cards.
Ctrl+Alt+F1
```
sudo apt-get install -f
``` wanted to remove ~400 packages so I said no thanks to that.
```
sudo envy -t
```
First selected the option to remove all the ATi stuff.
Then selected #6 clean install, but it threw all kind of complaints at me and didn't install anything.
Selected #5 and the options to install the latest.
Looks like it worked...
```
ct@wimp:/etc/X11$ lspci | grep VGA
01:00.0 VGA compatible controller: nVidia Corporation Unknown device 02e1 (rev a2)
ct@wimp:/etc/X11$ lspci -n | grep 0300
01:00.0 0300: 10de:02e1 (rev a2)
ct@wimp:/etc/X11$ glxinfo
OpenGL vendor string: NVIDIA Corporation
OpenGL renderer string: GeForce 7600 GS/AGP/SSE2
OpenGL version string: 2.1.1 NVIDIA 100.14.11
```
Don't know why it's a 'Unknown device' but as long as it works...

envy rocks!
Thanks again tseliot.

type:
```
sudo apt-get --purge remove envy
sudo rm -R /usr/share/envy

wget -c http://albertomilone.com/ubuntu/nvidia/scripts/envy_0.9.7-0ubuntu8_all.deb

sudo dpkg -i envy_0.9.7-0ubuntu8_all.deb

sudo apt-get install -f

```
then launch envy again

### Post by ddoxey on 2007-10-20
After the Gutsy update, my dual head ATI Radeon setup doesn't work any more.

I suspect these lines in my Xorg.0.log are of interest:
(II) RADEON(0): RandR 1.2 enabled, ignore the following RandR disabled message.
(WW) RADEON(0): Option "MergedFB" is not used
(WW) RADEON(0): Option "MonitorLayout" is not used
(WW) RADEON(0): Option "MetaModes" is not used
(--) RandR disabled
...

It appears that I'm now in the realm of XRandR configuration.

After Xfce starts, and I'm looking at my mirrored screens, I can fix it by executing;
**$ /usr/bin/xrandr --output VGA-0 --mode 1280x1024 --right-of DVI-0**

To make this automatic, I added it in the "Autostarted Applications" GUI tool.
The result is a mirrored screen configuration until Xfce is finished initializing, then things wink out briefly and return in dual screen mode.

This feels a lot like a crude workaround.
I tried adding it to .xinitrc and .xfce4-initrc. 
These didn't offer any results.

Any suggestions on an improved solution?

Thanks

        Identifier      "cursor"
        Driver          "wacom"
        Option          "Device"        "/dev/input/wacom"
        Option          "Type"  "cursor"
        Option          "ForceDevice"   "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
        Identifier      "PHILIPS 200P"
        Option          "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
        Identifier      "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
        Option          "VendorName"    "ATI Proprietary Driver"
        Option          "ModelName"     "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
        Option          "DPMS"  "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
        Identifier      "Generic Video Card"
        Driver          "fglrx"
        Busid           "PCI:1:0:0"
        Option          "VideoOverlay"  "on"
        Option          "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
EndSection

Section "Device"
        Identifier      "aticonfig-Device[0]"
        Driver          "fglrx"
        Option          "VideoOverlay"  "on"
        Option          "OpenGLOverlay" "off"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
        Identifier      "Default Screen"
        Device          "Generic Video Card"
        Monitor         "PHILIPS 200P"
        Defaultdepth    24
        SubSection "Display"
                Modes           "1600x1200"     "1280x1024"     "1024x768"      "832x624"    "800x600"       "720x480"       "720x400"       "640x480"       "640x400"
        EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Screen"
        Identifier      "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
        Device          "aticonfig-Device[0]"
        Monitor         "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
        Defaultdepth    24
        SubSection "Display"
                Viewport        0       0
                Depth   24
        EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
        Option          "Composite"     "Enable"
EndSection

---

### Post by gypojames on 2008-05-16
i have a ATI HD 2400 pro (pcie) card and a fresh 64bit hardy install and have had the same problems as others have had with aticonfig not configuring xorg.conf correctly (even by force command) with the latest driver from the ATI website and also when using envyng. after going into synaptic and enabling 'proposed' & 'unsupported' updates and then reloading, the update manager appeared on the taskbar and the latest ATI driver (8.47.3) was showing as an update. so after updating then running the 'sudo aticonfig --initial -f' command the xorg.conf was still not being configured correctly, so what i did was to add in all that was needed by hand through gediting the xorg.conf file. i took settings from other posts (taking out any comments with # before it) and thought i would post my xorg.conf incase anyone else is in the same frustrating position



# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load		"bitmap"
	Load		"vbe"
	Load		"ddc"
	Load		"freetype"
	Load		"int10"
	Load		"glx"
	Load		"dri"
	Load		"extmod"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Option	    "VendorName" "ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option	    "ModelName" "Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option	    "DPMS" "true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier  "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver      "fglrx"
        Option          "XAANoOffscreenPixmaps" "1"
        Option          "TexturedVideo" "1"
	Option		"TexturedVideoSync"	"1"
        Option          "BackingStore"  "1"
        Option          "Textured2D"    "1"
        Option          "TexturedXRender"       "1"
        Option          "BackingStore"  "1"
        Option          "AccelMethod"   "EXA"
        Option          "DRI"   "1"
        Option          "DynamicClocks" "1"
        Option          "ForceGenericGPU"       "0"
        Option          "VideoOverlay"  "0"
        Option          "OpenGLOverlay" "0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier "aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device     "aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor    "aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	DefaultDepth     24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
                Modes	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
		Depth     24
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
	Option		"AIGLX"	"1"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option		"RENDER"	"1"
	Option		"XVideo"	"1"
	Option		"Composite"	"1"
	Option		"Damage"	"1"
EndSection




other changes i added was to add the maximum screen resolutions for my monitor. so this can be changed accordingly by adding your own screen resolutions. the file doesnt list 'bus id' but seems not to be a problem for me as i have full compiz up and running and dont have any issues at all :) :guitar:

---
